title: Prototypes vs. Definitions
subtitle: Why 'Good Enough' Centers Beat Rigid Categories for Semantic Primitives

children.children: What if Schankian primitives like ATRANS aren't rigid definitions but fuzzy prototypes — 'good enough' centers in action-space where 'giving,' 'selling,' and 'stealing' cluster at varying distances? This tutorial explores how Eleanor Rosch's Prototype Theory transforms our understanding of semantic operators from brittle categories to adaptive, gradient representations.

props.title: 1. The Classical View: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions

children.children: Since Aristotle, the dominant theory of categorization has been the classical view: a concept is defined by a set of necessary and sufficient conditions. Something is a 'bird' if and only if it has feathers, lays eggs, has wings, etc. Every member is equally representative — a penguin is just as much a bird as a robin.

Schank's original Conceptual Dependency theory followed this pattern. ATRANS was defined as the transfer of an abstract relationship (like possession or control) between entities. Either something IS an ATRANS or it ISN'T. 'Give' is ATRANS. 'Kick' is not. Clean, binary, brittle.

Consider these sentences:

1. "John gave Mary the book" → clearly ATRANS (possession transfer)
2. "John sold Mary the book" → ATRANS + some economic context
3. "John lent Mary the book" → temporary ATRANS? Partial ATRANS?
4. "John showed Mary the book" → information transfer? Or just perception?
5. "John waved the book at Mary" → gestural... ATRANS-adjacent?

The classical view forces a binary decision at every boundary. But language doesn't have clean boundaries — it has gradients. This is where prototypes come in.

In the 1970s, cognitive psychologist Eleanor Rosch upended classical categorization with a simple observation: people consistently rate some category members as 'better examples' than others. A robin is a more typical bird than a penguin. A chair is a more typical piece of furniture than a telephone stand.

items.definition: The most central, representative member of a category — the cognitive 'best example.' Not necessarily a real instance, but an idealized abstraction.
The continuous spectrum from highly typical members (close to prototype) to atypical boundary cases (far from prototype). There is no sharp cutoff.
Wittgenstein's insight that category members share overlapping features without any single feature being universal. Like members of a family who all 'look alike' without sharing one trait.

children.children: Think of a city. There's a recognizable 'downtown' — the center. As you move outward, the city gradually becomes suburbs, then countryside. There's no exact point where the city 'ends.' People near the center clearly live 'in the city.' People at the edge might disagree about whether they're in or out. The prototype is downtown; typicality is distance from center.

Rosch's key experimental findings: (1) People respond faster to typical members ('Is a robin a bird?' → fast YES) than atypical ones ('Is a penguin a bird?' → slow YES). (2) Children learn typical members first. (3) Typical members are listed first in free recall. (4) Typical members serve as cognitive reference points — we say 'a penguin is a kind of bird' but never 'a robin is a kind of bird' because robin IS the reference.

props.title: 3. ATRANS as a Prototype in Action-Space

children.children: Now apply Rosch's framework to Schankian primitives. Instead of ATRANS being a rigid definition ('transfer of abstract relation'), it becomes a prototype — a central point in action-space with verbs at varying distances:

Imagine ATRANS as the center of a bullseye:

🎯 Core (distance ≈ 0): "give" — pure, prototypical possession transfer

🔵 Inner ring (distance ≈ 0.2): "donate", "hand over", "deliver" — clear transfer, minor contextual variation

🟢 Middle ring (distance ≈ 0.5): "sell", "trade", "buy" — transfer + economic reciprocity

🟡 Outer ring (distance ≈ 0.7): "lend", "borrow", "rent" — temporary/conditional transfer

🟠 Boundary zone (distance ≈ 0.9): "steal", "confiscate", "inherit" — non-consensual or involuntary transfer

🔴 Disputed territory (distance > 1.0): "show", "share (digitally)", "influence" — metaphorical or partial transfer

This gradient view solves the boundary problem. We don't need to argue whether 'lending' is or isn't ATRANS — it's 70% ATRANS-like. The model can work with that continuous similarity rather than forcing a binary classification.

props.title: Why Distances Matter More Than Boundaries

children.children:
In a classical system, you need rules for every edge case: 'Is lending ATRANS?' requires a committee decision. In a prototype system, the edge cases take care of themselves — lending is automatically 0.7 ATRANS because its embedding lands there. This means:

• No hand-crafted boundary rules needed
• Graceful degradation: novel verbs get reasonable classifications by proximity
• Cross-linguistic flexibility: different languages can have slightly different prototype centers while sharing the same gradient structure
• Metaphor handling: 'she gave him a dirty look' naturally lands far from ATRANS center, toward MTRANS, without needing a special metaphor module

props.title: 4. From Prototypes to Embeddings: The Computational Connection

children.children: The connection between prototype theory and modern embeddings is deep. Word2Vec, GloVe, and transformer representations implicitly create prototype-like structures. Recent work (arXiv:2509.19269) shows LLMs can extract conceptual spaces via prototype embeddings — the mathematical structure Rosch hypothesized is actually learnable.

Here's how you could extract Schankian prototypes from an LLM:

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans

# Step 1: Collect verb embeddings from LLM
verbs = ["give", "sell", "lend", "steal", "donate",
         "hit", "kick", "punch", "slap", "shoot",
         "tell", "inform", "explain", "teach", "lie"]

# Get contextual embeddings (average over many contexts)
embeddings = get_verb_embeddings(verbs, model="llama-3")

# Step 2: Cluster to find prototype centers
K = 14  # Schank's original number of primitives
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=K)
kmeans.fit(embeddings)

# Step 3: Each cluster center IS a learned prototype
prototypes = kmeans.cluster_centers_  # shape: (14, d)

# Step 4: Typicality = inverse distance to nearest prototype
def typicality(verb_embedding, prototype):
    dist = np.linalg.norm(verb_embedding - prototype)
    return 1.0 / (1.0 + dist)  # sigmoid-like

# "give" should have high typicality for ATRANS prototype
# "lend" should have moderate typicality for ATRANS
# "kick" should have near-zero typicality for ATRANS
```

The key insight: we don't define ATRANS and then classify verbs. We let the data reveal prototypical clusters, and ATRANS is whatever cluster 'give/sell/donate' naturally form.

⚠️ Simple K-means over verb embeddings won't recover clean Schankian primitives — context matters enormously. 'She shot him a glance' and 'She shot him dead' use the same verb but belong to completely different operator clusters. You need contextualized embeddings that capture the full event, not just the verb in isolation.

props.title: 5. Rosch's Two Principles in Operator Design

children.children: Rosch identified two fundamental principles governing category structure. Both have direct implications for how we design operator systems:

Categories exist to reduce the infinite variety of experience to a manageable number of equivalence classes. The mind doesn't store 10,000 separate verb concepts — it stores ~14 prototypical action patterns and positions everything else relative to them.

For operators: This justifies a SMALL set of primitives. If you have 200 operators, you've lost the cognitive economy that makes primitives useful. The ideal set is the one that maximizes coverage while minimizing redundancy — exactly what Minimum Description Length (MDL) formalizes.

Prediction: The optimal number of Schankian operators should be roughly log₂(N) where N is the number of distinct event types in a corpus. For natural language with ~5000 common event patterns, that's about 12-13 operators — remarkably close to Schank's original 11-14!

Categories mirror real structure in the world. High-correlations among features create natural clusters: things that fly tend to have feathers, wings, light bones. The world is not uniformly distributed — it has clumps.

For operators: This means Schankian primitives should emerge from data because the WORLD has genuine event structure. 'Transfer of possession' is a real recurring pattern, not an arbitrary linguistic convention. Different cultures have different economies and social structures, but the PHYSICS of transfer (something moves from A to B) is universal.

This is why we expect cross-linguistic convergence: different languages are sampling the same underlying world structure, and that structure has natural prototypes.

The deepest insight from prototype theory is that categories don't need to be perfect — they need to be 'good enough.' A prototype that captures 80% of the variance in a cluster is more useful than a definition that gets 100% of the easy cases but fails on every edge case.

Your GPS doesn't compute the mathematically optimal route. It computes a 'good enough' route using heuristics. And it works because: (1) the approximate route gets you within the right neighborhood, (2) local adjustments handle the fine details, (3) an approximate answer NOW is better than a perfect answer NEVER. Similarly, a prototype-based operator that's approximately right for 'lend' is infinitely more useful than a classical definition system that can't categorize 'lend' at all because it doesn't perfectly match any rule.

This has a profound architectural implication: we should train operators to maximize COVERAGE (how many events they can reasonably approximate) rather than PRECISION (how perfectly they classify each event). A slightly fuzzy ATRANS that handles give/sell/lend/steal/donate/inherit/confiscate is vastly more powerful than a precise ATRANS that only handles give/donate.

props.title: 7. Basic Level Categories: The Goldilocks Zone

children.children: Rosch discovered that there's a privileged level of abstraction — the 'basic level' — where categories are most useful. Not too abstract (furniture), not too specific (kitchen chair), but just right (chair). Basic level categories maximize within-category similarity and between-category difference.

props.title: Finding the Basic Level for Operators

children.children:
Consider three levels of abstraction for a 'hitting' event:

🔺 Superordinate: CAUSE_CHANGE — too abstract, covers everything from painting to demolition

✅ Basic Level: PROPEL — just right, captures 'exerting physical force on an object'

🔻 Subordinate: PUNCH_WITH_RIGHT_FIST — too specific, fails to generalize

Schank's original primitives were designed at the basic level, but intuitively. The prototype framework gives us a METRIC for finding it:

```
basic_level_score(category) = 
    avg_within_similarity(category) 
    - avg_between_similarity(category, neighbors)
```

The category level that maximizes this score is the basic level — the Goldilocks zone where operators are most informative.

This connects directly to the Hierarchical Operator Trees idea (brainstorm #10): the tree has superordinate operators at the top, subordinate at the bottom, and the basic level — where Schankian primitives live — in the middle.

props.title: 8. Graded Membership and Soft Operator Assignment

children.children: In a prototype system, every event has a DEGREE of membership in every operator category. This enables soft operator assignment — a single event can partially activate multiple operators, with weights proportional to typicality.

"The teacher explained the theorem to the students."

Soft operator assignment:
• MTRANS (mental transfer):  0.65 — primary: information moved from teacher to students
• MBUILD (mental construction): 0.25 — secondary: students construct understanding  
• ATRANS (abstract transfer):  0.08 — weak: some 'possession of knowledge' transfer metaphor
• ATTEND (focus attention):   0.02 — trace: students direct attention

Total: 1.00 (softmax normalization)

This is richer than saying 'explain = MTRANS.' The soft assignment captures the MULTI-OPERATOR nature of complex events, weighted by typicality to each prototype.
💡 Soft operator assignment is exactly what a Mixture of Experts (MoE) router computes! See brainstorm #62 for how MoE architectures naturally implement prototype-based operator selection.

Prototypes aren't fixed — they drift over time and vary across cultures. The prototypical 'bird' in Antarctica might be a penguin. The prototypical 'transfer' in a gift economy looks different from one in a market economy.

props.title: What Prototype Drift Means for Universal Operators

children.children:
If Schankian operators are truly universal, their prototypes should be stable across languages and cultures — at least at the basic level. But the TYPICALITY GRADIENTS around them can vary:

• In English, 'give' is the prototypical ATRANS
• In Japanese, the distinction between あげる (ageru, giving upward) and くれる (kureru, giving toward speaker) means the prototype might be MORE specific — directional giving
• In Pirahã (which lacks number words), ATRANS might center on direct hand-to-hand exchange

The key prediction: prototype CENTERS converge across languages (all have something like 'transfer'), but prototype BOUNDARIES vary (what counts as 'close enough' to transfer differs). This is testable! Compare verb clusters across parallel corpora.

props.question: In Rosch's framework, why is a robin considered more 'typical' of the category 'bird' than a penguin?
options: Robins are more common in nature than penguins
Robins share more features with other birds (flight, small size, perching) creating higher family resemblance
Robins were discovered first and got priority
The definition of 'bird' was written with robins in mind
props.explanation: Typicality is about feature overlap with other category members — family resemblance. Robins fly, perch, are small, sing — features shared by many birds. Penguins don't fly, swim instead, are large — fewer shared features. It's not about frequency or historical priority, but about centrality in feature space.

props.question: What is the main advantage of treating ATRANS as a prototype rather than a definition?
options: It makes the system faster to compute
It eliminates the need for any operator categories
It handles boundary cases (like 'lending') gracefully via continuous distance rather than forced binary classification
props.explanation: The prototype approach replaces brittle binary decisions with gradient membership. 'Lending' doesn't need a committee ruling — it's automatically positioned at ~0.7 similarity to the ATRANS prototype. This handles edge cases, novel verbs, and cross-linguistic variation naturally.

props.question: Rosch's 'basic level' categories are special because they:
options: Are the most abstract level possible
Maximize within-category similarity AND between-category difference
Are always exactly one word in every language
props.explanation: Basic level categories hit the Goldilocks zone: members are similar to each other (high within-category similarity) while being distinct from members of neighboring categories (high between-category difference). This is where Schankian operators live — abstract enough to generalize, concrete enough to be informative.

props.question: If 'The teacher explained the theorem to the students' gets soft operator assignment of MTRANS: 0.65, MBUILD: 0.25, ATRANS: 0.08, ATTEND: 0.02 — what does this tell us?
options: The sentence is ambiguous and the system can't decide
Only MTRANS matters; the rest are noise
The event is primarily information transfer but also involves knowledge construction, with traces of other operator patterns
The system needs more training data
props.explanation: Soft assignment captures the multi-faceted nature of real events. 'Explaining' is PRIMARILY mental transfer (MTRANS) but also involves the students building understanding (MBUILD). The small ATRANS component reflects the metaphor of 'possessing' knowledge. This is richer than any single-label classification.

children.children: The prototype view of operators connects to many other ideas in this project:

🔗 Connections:

• MDL Operator Discovery (#45): The 'right' number of prototypes is the one that minimizes description length — cognitive economy formalized
• Hierarchical Operator Trees (#10): Basic level = the tree layer where prototypes live; superordinate and subordinate are above and below
• MoE Routing (#62): Soft operator assignment IS mixture-of-experts routing, with prototypes as expert specializations
• Information Bottleneck (#39): Prototypes emerge naturally from IB compression — they're the features that survive the bottleneck
• Semantic Renormalization (#47): Coarse-graining flows toward prototypes; prototypes ARE the fixed points of the RG flow

The deepest implication: if operators are prototypes, then the entire question of 'what are the right primitives?' transforms. We're not looking for the ONE TRUE set of definitions. We're looking for the natural cluster structure of event-space — and different levels of granularity give different (equally valid) sets. The universe doesn't come pre-labeled. Our job is to find the 'good enough' centers that make reasoning tractable.
